title: Sedan Engine Overheating — Diagnosis & Repair Guide
content: Symptoms: Temperature gauge in the red zone, steam from hood, coolant warning light, sweet smell of coolant. Diagnostic Steps: 1) Check coolant level in the reservoir — if low, inspect for leaks at hose clamps, water pump weep hole, and radiator seams. 2) Test the thermostat by removing it and placing in boiling water — it should open at 195°F. 3) Inspect the radiator cap for a worn seal (should hold 13-16 PSI). 4) Run a cooling system pressure test to locate hidden leaks. 5) Check the electric cooling fan relay and fuse. Repair Procedure: Replace the thermostat ($15-$30 part, 1 hour labor). If the water pump is leaking, replace it along with the timing belt if applicable ($300-$600 total). Flush the cooling system and refill with manufacturer-specified coolant mix (50/50). Parts Needed: Thermostat, thermostat gasket, coolant, radiator cap. Estimated Cost: $50-$600 depending on root cause. Prevention: Flush coolant every 30,000 miles or 2 years. Inspect hoses for cracks and swelling at every oil change.

title: Sedan Brake Pad & Rotor Replacement Guide
content: Symptoms: Squealing or grinding noise when braking, longer stopping distances, brake pedal pulsation, vehicle pulling to one side. Diagnostic Steps: 1) Visual inspection through wheel spokes — pads should be at least 3mm thick. 2) Remove the wheel and measure pad thickness with a caliper. 3) Inspect rotors for scoring, hot spots (blue discoloration), and measure thickness against minimum spec (usually stamped on rotor). 4) Check brake fluid level and color — dark fluid indicates contamination. 5) Inspect brake lines for leaks or corrosion. Repair Procedure: Jack up vehicle and secure on jack stands. Remove caliper bolts (usually 14mm), hang caliper with wire — never let it hang by the hose. Remove old pads, compress the piston with a C-clamp (open bleeder valve on rear calipers to prevent ABS damage). If rotors are below minimum thickness or heavily scored, replace them. Install new pads with brake grease on contact points (NOT the friction surface). Torque wheels to 80-100 ft-lbs. Pump brake pedal before driving. Parts Needed: Brake pads (front set $30-$60), rotors if needed ($40-$80 each), brake cleaner, brake grease. Estimated Cost: $150-$400 per axle. Prevention: Inspect pads every 15,000 miles. Avoid aggressive braking. Flush brake fluid every 2 years.

title: Sedan Oil Leak Detection & Repair
content: Symptoms: Oil spots under the vehicle, burning oil smell, low oil level between changes, blue exhaust smoke. Diagnostic Steps: 1) Clean the engine with degreaser and run for 15 minutes to identify the fresh leak source. 2) Common leak points: valve cover gasket, oil pan gasket, rear main seal, oil filter housing, oil cooler lines. 3) Use UV dye and a blacklight for hard-to-find leaks — add dye to oil, run engine 20 minutes, inspect with UV light. 4) Check PCV valve — a stuck PCV creates excessive crankcase pressure causing gasket failures. Repair Procedure: For valve cover gasket (most common): Remove coil packs/plug wires, unbolt valve cover, clean mating surfaces thoroughly, install new gasket with a thin bead of RTV at corners, torque bolts in sequence to 8-10 ft-lbs. For oil pan gasket: May require lifting the engine slightly for clearance. Parts Needed: Valve cover gasket set ($15-$40), RTV sealant, oil pan gasket ($20-$50), new oil and filter. Estimated Cost: $100-$500. Prevention: Use manufacturer-recommended oil weight. Replace PCV valve every 30,000 miles. Don't overfill oil.

title: Sedan Automatic Transmission — Slipping & Failure Prevention
content: Symptoms: RPMs rise without corresponding acceleration (slipping), delayed or harsh shifts, transmission warning light, burning smell from transmission fluid, fluid leak (red/brown fluid). Diagnostic Steps: 1) Check transmission fluid with engine running in Park — should be pink/red and not smell burnt. Dark brown or black fluid with a burnt smell indicates internal damage. 2) Scan for diagnostic trouble codes (P0700-P0799 range). 3) Perform a stall test — in Drive with brakes held, RPMs should not exceed 2,200-2,500. Higher indicates slipping. 4) Check for external leaks at the pan gasket, cooler lines, and axle seals. Repair Procedure: If caught early (fluid discoloration only): Perform a complete fluid and filter change — drop the pan, replace the filter, clean the magnets, install new pan gasket, refill with manufacturer-specified ATF. For internal damage (slipping, hard shifts): A transmission rebuild ($1,500-$3,500) or replacement ($2,500-$5,000) is required. Parts Needed: ATF fluid (8-12 quarts), transmission filter kit, pan gasket. Estimated Cost: $150-$300 for service, $1,500-$5,000 for rebuild/replace. Prevention: Change transmission fluid every 40,000 miles. Never tow heavy loads with a sedan. Allow the engine to warm up before aggressive driving.

title: Sedan Battery & Charging System Diagnostics
content: Symptoms: Slow engine crank, dim headlights, dashboard warning light, clicking noise when turning key, electronics malfunctioning. Diagnostic Steps: 1) Test battery voltage with multimeter — should read 12.4-12.7V with engine off. Below 12.2V indicates a weak battery. 2) Load test the battery — voltage should not drop below 9.6V under load. 3) With engine running, voltage should read 13.7-14.7V — if not, the alternator is suspect. 4) Inspect battery terminals for corrosion (white/green buildup). 5) Check the serpentine belt for proper tension — a slipping belt reduces alternator output. Repair Procedure: Clean terminals with a mixture of baking soda and water, wire brush the posts. If battery fails load test, replace it — disconnect negative terminal first, then positive. Install new battery (positive first, then negative). Apply terminal grease to prevent corrosion. If the alternator is faulty, replace it — release belt tension, disconnect wiring harness, swap unit. Parts Needed: Battery ($100-$200), terminal cleaner, terminal grease, alternator if needed ($200-$400). Estimated Cost: $100-$600. Prevention: Test battery before winter. Inspect terminals at every oil change. Replace battery every 4-5 years proactively.

title: Sedan Tire Wear Analysis & Replacement Guide
content: Symptoms: Uneven tread wear, vibration at highway speeds, vehicle pulling to one side, visible tread wear indicators (bars flush with tread surface), cracking on sidewalls. Diagnostic Steps: 1) Use the penny test — insert a penny head-first into tread grooves. If you see all of Lincoln's head, tread is below 2/32" and tires must be replaced. 2) Inspect wear patterns: center wear = overinflation, edge wear = underinflation, one-side wear = alignment issue, cupping/scalloping = worn shocks/struts. 3) Check tire pressure against the door jamb sticker (NOT the tire sidewall max). 4) Inspect for bulges, cuts, or embedded objects. Repair Procedure: Replace tires in pairs (same axle minimum) or all four. Mount and balance new tires. Perform a 4-wheel alignment ($80-$120). If wear was caused by worn suspension components, replace those first — otherwise new tires will wear prematurely. Parts Needed: Tires ($80-$200 each for sedan), valve stems, wheel weights. Estimated Cost: $400-$900 for a full set with alignment. Prevention: Rotate tires every 5,000-7,500 miles. Check pressure monthly. Get alignment checked annually or after hitting a significant pothole.

title: Sedan Suspension — Strut & Shock Replacement
content: Symptoms: Bouncy ride, nose diving during braking, excessive body roll in turns, clunking over bumps, uneven tire wear (cupping pattern), visible oil leaking from strut body. Diagnostic Steps: 1) Bounce test — push down on each corner of the vehicle firmly and release. It should return to rest within 1-2 bounces. Continued bouncing indicates worn shocks/struts. 2) Visual inspection — look for oil on the strut body or shock tube. 3) Inspect strut mounts for cracks and bearing play (turn the wheel lock-to-lock and listen for popping). 4) Check sway bar end links for play — grab and shake by hand. Repair Procedure: Replace struts in pairs (both fronts or both rears). Use a spring compressor to transfer the spring to the new strut, or buy quick-strut assemblies that include the spring, mount, and bearing pre-assembled ($150-$250 each). Torque all fasteners to spec. Alignment is REQUIRED after strut replacement. Parts Needed: Strut assemblies ($100-$250 each), sway bar end links ($20-$40 each), alignment. Estimated Cost: $500-$1,200 per axle installed. Prevention: Avoid potholes and speed bumps at high speed. Replace struts every 50,000-75,000 miles.

title: SUV Engine Overheating — Heavy-Duty Cooling System Repair
content: Symptoms: Temperature gauge climbing during towing or uphill driving, coolant overflow, AC losing effectiveness when engine is hot, check engine light with P0217 code. Diagnostic Steps: 1) SUVs generate more heat due to larger engines and heavier loads — check if the issue occurs only under load (towing, climbing). 2) Inspect the auxiliary transmission cooler — integrated cooler-in-radiator designs can fail, leaking ATF into coolant (milky appearance). 3) Test both electric cooling fans — many SUVs have dual fans; one may have failed. 4) Check the coolant condition — use test strips for pH and freeze point. 5) Inspect the water pump for weep-hole leaks — SUV water pumps handle higher flow rates and wear faster. Repair Procedure: If only one fan is failed, replace the fan motor or relay ($100-$250). For a compromised radiator (ATF contamination), replace the radiator AND flush the transmission cooler circuit ($400-$800). Replace the water pump and thermostat as a set if the vehicle is over 80K miles ($350-$700). Parts Needed: Radiator ($200-$400), water pump ($80-$150), thermostat, cooling fans, coolant (2-3 gallons). Estimated Cost: $200-$1,200. Prevention: Use tow/haul mode when towing. Add an auxiliary transmission cooler for heavy towing. Flush coolant every 30,000 miles.

title: SUV Heavy-Duty Brake Service & Upgrade Guide
content: Symptoms: Brake fade during mountain driving or towing, longer stopping distances, brake dust accumulation, pedal vibration, ABS light activation. Diagnostic Steps: 1) SUV brakes work harder due to higher vehicle weight (4,500-6,000 lbs) — inspect pads at 20,000-mile intervals. 2) Measure rotor thickness — SUV rotors are thicker but have higher minimum thickness specs. 3) Check brake fluid boiling point with a refractometer — if below 400°F, fluid has absorbed moisture and needs replacement. 4) Inspect rear brake drum/shoes if equipped — many SUVs use rear drums. 5) Test the brake booster vacuum — engine off, pump brakes 3 times, hold pedal and start engine; pedal should drop. Repair Procedure: Always use heavy-duty rated pads (ceramic or semi-metallic rated for towing). Replace rotors rather than resurfacing on SUVs — the extra thickness margin is needed for heat dissipation. If the vehicle tows regularly, upgrade to slotted rotors for better heat management. Bleed brake system after any component replacement. Parts Needed: Heavy-duty brake pads ($50-$90 per axle), rotors ($60-$120 each), brake fluid (DOT 3 or 4 per spec), brake hardware kit. Estimated Cost: $300-$700 per axle. Prevention: Use engine braking on long descents. Allow brakes to cool after towing. Flush fluid every 24 months.

title: SUV Oil Leak — Common Sources & Repair Procedures
content: Symptoms: Oil spots on driveway (may be larger volume due to bigger engines), low oil warnings, burning smell especially after off-road driving, oil on skid plate. Diagnostic Steps: 1) SUVs with higher ground clearance can accumulate road debris that damages the oil pan — inspect the pan for dents and cracks. 2) Check the oil cooler and its lines — many SUV engines use external oil coolers that develop leaks at the gaskets. 3) Inspect the front and rear crankshaft seals — the front seal is behind the harmonic balancer, the rear seal is between the engine and transmission. 4) On 4WD models, check the transfer case seals and the front differential. Repair Procedure: Oil pan leaks: Clean and reseal with high-temp RTV, or replace the gasket and pan if dented. Oil cooler gasket: Remove cooler housing, clean surfaces, install new O-rings or gaskets — do NOT reuse old ones. Rear main seal: This requires transmission removal — budget for significant labor ($800-$1,500 labor alone). Parts Needed: Oil pan gasket ($25-$60), oil cooler gaskets ($15-$30), rear main seal ($20-$40), RTV sealant. Estimated Cost: $100-$2,000 depending on source. Prevention: Install a skid plate for off-road use. Change oil at manufacturer intervals. Inspect undercarriage after off-road excursions.

title: SUV Transmission — 4WD/AWD System Diagnosis & Repair
content: Symptoms: Grinding noise when engaging 4WD, difficulty shifting between 2WD/4WD, vibration at speed in 4WD, check engine or 4WD warning lights, transfer case fluid leak. Diagnostic Steps: 1) Check transfer case fluid level and condition — should be clean and at proper level. Low fluid causes accelerated wear. 2) Scan for transfer case and transmission DTCs — common codes include P0833 (clutch position) and P0841 (fluid pressure). 3) Inspect the front and rear driveshafts for U-joint play — grab the shaft and check for movement. 4) Test the 4WD actuator — listen for engagement when switching modes. 5) Check for binding during tight turns in 4WD on dry pavement (this is normal for part-time 4WD — do NOT use part-time 4WD on dry pavement). Repair Procedure: Transfer case fluid change: Drain and refill with manufacturer-specified fluid (often a special ATF or proprietary fluid). For worn U-joints: Replace the entire driveshaft if U-joints are non-serviceable, or press in new U-joints ($20-$40 each). For a failing transfer case: Rebuild ($800-$1,500) or replace ($1,200-$3,000). Transmission service: Full fluid and filter change every 40,000 miles. Parts Needed: Transfer case fluid, transmission fluid, U-joints, transfer case motor/actuator ($150-$400). Estimated Cost: $100-$3,500. Prevention: Change transfer case fluid every 30,000 miles. Never use part-time 4WD on dry pavement. Engage 4WD monthly to keep seals lubricated.

title: SUV Suspension — Lift Kit, Air Suspension & Component Repair
content: Symptoms: Vehicle sagging on one side, air compressor running constantly (air suspension models), rough ride over bumps, steering wander, death wobble at highway speeds. Diagnostic Steps: 1) Measure ride height at all four corners — compare to manufacturer specs. Difference of more than 0.5 inches indicates a problem. 2) For air suspension SUVs: Listen for the compressor running more than 30 seconds — indicates a leak. Spray soapy water on air springs to find bubbles. 3) Inspect ball joints by jacking up the front and checking for vertical play with a pry bar. 4) Check control arm bushings for cracking and separation. 5) Test steering stabilizer for leaks and proper damping. Repair Procedure: Upper and lower ball joints: Press out old, press in new. Requires a ball joint press tool ($40 rental). Replace both sides even if only one is worn. Control arm bushings: Often easier to replace the entire control arm assembly ($80-$200 each). Air springs: Replace the leaking air spring bag ($150-$400 each) — consider converting to conventional coil springs ($600-$1,000 kit) for long-term reliability. Parts Needed: Ball joints ($30-$80 each), control arms ($80-$200 each), air springs or coil conversion kit, steering stabilizer ($50-$100). Estimated Cost: $400-$2,000. Prevention: Inspect suspension components every 30,000 miles. Avoid overloading. Reduce speed on rough terrain.

title: SUV Battery & Dual-Battery System Diagnostics
content: Symptoms: Slow cranking especially in cold weather, accessories losing power, battery warning light, infotainment system resetting, remote start failing. Diagnostic Steps: 1) SUVs often have larger batteries (group 48/94R) due to higher electrical demands — test with a load tester rated for the battery's CCA rating. 2) Check parasitic draw — SUVs with many modules (4WD, power seats, infotainment) can have parasitic draws over 50mA that kill batteries. Use a multimeter in series with the negative terminal and pull fuses one at a time to isolate the draw. 3) For diesel SUVs with dual batteries: Test both batteries individually and check the isolator relay. 4) Inspect the battery tray for corrosion — SUV batteries sit lower and are exposed to more moisture. Repair Procedure: Replace battery with correct group size and CCA rating (typically 700-850 CCA). If parasitic draw exceeds 50mA, isolate the circuit and repair the module (common culprits: aftermarket stereos, dash cams, seat modules that don't sleep). Clean battery tray and apply rust inhibitor. Parts Needed: Battery ($150-$250), terminal ends, battery tray, anti-corrosion washers. Estimated Cost: $150-$400. Prevention: Test battery before winter and summer. Turn off accessories before shutting off engine. If vehicle sits for extended periods, use a battery maintainer.

title: SUV Tire Wear — All-Terrain & Highway Tire Maintenance
content: Symptoms: Excessive road noise (feathered wear pattern), vibration, reduced off-road traction, sidewall cracking (especially on all-terrain tires), cupping wear on highway tires. Diagnostic Steps: 1) SUV tires wear differently based on 2WD vs 4WD/AWD configuration — AWD vehicles MUST have matching tread depth on all four tires (within 2/32") to avoid transfer case damage. 2) Measure tread depth at inside, center, and outside — SUV tires often wear on the inside edge due to alignment and weight. 3) Check for scalloping/cupping — indicates worn struts or unbalanced tires. 4) Inspect for sidewall damage — all-terrain tires are stiffer but can suffer sidewall punctures off-road. Repair Procedure: Replace tires in sets of 4 on AWD vehicles to protect the drivetrain. On 4WD vehicles, pairs are acceptable if the other pair has sufficient tread. Use tires rated for the vehicle's load rating (check the GVWR on the door jamb). After mounting, perform a 4-wheel alignment and rebalance. Parts Needed: SUV tires ($120-$300 each), valve stems, alignment. Estimated Cost: $600-$1,400 for a full set. Prevention: Rotate every 5,000 miles. Match tire sizes on AWD exactly. Check pressure weekly when switching between highway and off-road use. Air down for off-road, re-inflate for highway.

title: Truck Engine Overheating — Heavy-Load Cooling System Service
content: Symptoms: Overheating when hauling payloads or towing trailers, coolant loss through overflow, reduced heater output, check engine light with temperature codes. Diagnostic Steps: 1) Trucks frequently overheat due to heavy workloads — verify the payload/tow rating is not being exceeded. 2) Inspect the intercooler (turbocharged trucks) — debris and bugs can block airflow. Clean with low-pressure water. 3) Check the EGR cooler (diesel trucks) — a failing EGR cooler leaks exhaust into the cooling system, causing overheating and coolant loss. Look for white exhaust smoke. 4) Test the fan clutch — spin the fan with the engine off; it should resist with 1-2 turns. Free spinning means the clutch is worn. 5) On trucks with mechanical fans, check the fan shroud — a missing or damaged shroud reduces cooling efficiency by 30-40%. Repair Procedure: Fan clutch replacement: Remove the fan and clutch assembly (usually 36mm nut, reverse thread). Install new fan clutch ($100-$250). EGR cooler (diesel): Remove intake manifold, replace the EGR cooler assembly ($300-$600 part). Flush the cooling system thoroughly after EGR cooler failure to remove exhaust contamination. Parts Needed: Fan clutch ($100-$250), EGR cooler ($300-$600), coolant (3-4 gallons for truck engines), radiator hoses. Estimated Cost: $200-$1,500. Prevention: Clean the radiator and intercooler annually. Don't exceed tow ratings. Use tow/haul driving mode.

title: Truck Brake System — Heavy-Duty Inspection & Service
content: Symptoms: Brake fade when loaded or descending grades, excessive stopping distance, trailer sway during braking, ABS activation on dry pavement, burning smell after heavy braking. Diagnostic Steps: 1) Measure pad thickness — truck pads wear faster under load. Replace at 4mm minimum (trucks need the extra margin). 2) Check for brake proportioning issues — loaded trucks shift weight to the rear, unloaded they're front-heavy. Verify the load-sensing proportioning valve is functioning. 3) Inspect the exhaust brake/engine brake (diesel trucks) — a non-functional engine brake puts all stopping load on the service brakes. 4) For trucks with electric trailer brake controller: Test output amperage at the 7-pin connector with the trailer connected. 5) Check drum brakes on the rear axle — many trucks use drums rated for higher loads. Repair Procedure: Use severe-duty or towing-rated brake pads — standard pads will fade under load. Replace rotors in pairs and ensure they meet or exceed OEM thickness specs. Service drum brakes: Replace shoes, check wheel cylinder for leaks, inspect hardware and return springs, adjust star wheel. Test trailer brake gain setting (should be set per trailer weight). Parts Needed: Heavy-duty brake pads ($60-$120 per axle), rotors ($80-$150 each), drum shoes ($40-$80), wheel cylinders ($20-$40), brake hardware kit. Estimated Cost: $400-$1,000 per axle. Prevention: Use engine braking on long descents. Don't ride brakes. Adjust trailer brake gain for each load.

title: Truck Oil Leak — Diesel & Gas Engine Common Sources
content: Symptoms: Large oil spots (truck engines hold 7-10 quarts), rapid oil consumption between changes, oil on the exhaust manifold causing smoke, oil dripping from bell housing (rear main seal). Diagnostic Steps: 1) Diesel trucks: Check the turbo oil feed and return lines — turbo seal failure pushes oil into the intake or exhaust. Blue/white smoke confirm this. 2) Inspect the oil cooler and its gaskets — especially on trucks with towing packages that have external oil coolers. 3) Check the oil filter adapter housing — the O-ring or gasket for the filter housing is a common leak source on modern truck engines. 4) Rear main seal: Oil at the bottom of the bell housing between engine and transmission. 5) On trucks with engine oil cooler integrated into the block, inspect for coolant mixing (milky oil or oily coolant). Repair Procedure: Turbo oil lines: Replace the braided supply and rubber return lines with new gaskets ($50-$100). Oil filter housing gasket: Replace the O-ring ($5-$15 part, 30 minutes labor). Rear main seal: Requires transmission removal — on trucks this is significant labor ($1,000-$2,000 labor). Consider replacing the clutch (manual) or flex plate inspection (automatic) while the transmission is out. Parts Needed: Turbo oil lines ($50-$100), oil filter housing O-ring ($5-$15), rear main seal ($25-$50). Estimated Cost: $50-$2,500. Prevention: Use the correct oil weight for the climate. Change oil at or before manufacturer intervals. Inspect turbo lines during every oil change on diesel trucks.

title: Truck Transmission — HD Automatic & Manual Gearbox Service
content: Symptoms: Slipping under load (especially in higher gears when towing), harsh 1-2 shift, delayed reverse engagement, transmission temperature warning, manual transmission grinding or popping out of gear. Diagnostic Steps: 1) Check transmission fluid level and condition — trucks that tow should have fluid changed every 30,000 miles. 2) Scan for transmission DTCs — solenoid failures are common on electronically controlled truck transmissions. 3) Test line pressure with a gauge — low pressure indicates a weak pump or worn seals. 4) For manual transmissions: Synchros wear first in the most-used gears (2nd and 3rd). Grinding during shifts indicates synchro wear. 5) Check the torque converter lockup — it should engage above 40 mph. Failure causes overheating and slipping. Repair Procedure: For automatic: If caught early, a fluid change, new filter, and solenoid pack replacement ($300-$700) can resolve shift quality issues. For significant slipping, plan for a rebuild with upgraded clutch packs (HD build: $2,500-$4,500). For manual: Synchro replacement requires transmission removal and disassembly ($1,500-$2,500). If the clutch hasn't been replaced recently, do it simultaneously ($400-$800 additional). Parts Needed: ATF (12-16 quarts for truck transmission), filter, solenoid pack, clutch kit (manual). Estimated Cost: $300-$5,000. Prevention: Install an auxiliary transmission cooler. Never exceed GCWR. Change fluid every 30,000 miles when towing regularly.

title: Truck Tire — Load Rating, Wear & Replacement Protocol
content: Symptoms: Rear tires wearing faster than fronts (rear-wheel drive bias), irregular wear on inside edge (heavy loads), sidewall bulging (overloaded), dually inner and outer tire wear discrepancy. Diagnostic Steps: 1) Verify tire load rating matches the truck's GVWR — use Load Range E (10-ply) minimum for heavy-duty use. 2) Inspect dually tires: Inner tires are often neglected — use a pressure gauge extension to check them. Mismatched circumference between inner/outer causes scrubbing wear. 3) Measure tread depth at multiple points — loaded trucks cause center wear on rear tires. 4) Check for flat spots from sitting with heavy loads — common on work trucks parked over weekends. 5) Inspect for embedded objects from job sites (nails, screws, rocks). Repair Procedure: Replace all tires on the same axle at the same time. For dually setups, replace all four rear tires simultaneously. Match brand, model, and tread depth within 2/32". Use a torque wrench on lug nuts — over-torquing warps rotors, under-torquing is a safety hazard (typical spec: 120-140 ft-lbs). After mounting, perform a 4-wheel alignment with the truck at typical operating weight. Parts Needed: LT tires ($150-$350 each), valve stems, alignment. Estimated Cost: $600-$2,400 for a full set. Prevention: Rotate every 5,000-7,500 miles. Match pressure to load (check door placard for loaded and unloaded pressures). Inspect dually inner tires at every service.

title: Truck Suspension — Leaf Spring, Leveling Kit & Component Service
content: Symptoms: Rear sag when loaded, rough ride empty (over-sprung), leaf spring squeaking, cab vibration, steering wander after loading. Diagnostic Steps: 1) Inspect leaf springs for broken leaves — a cracked leaf can be heard as a clunk over bumps. 2) Measure rear ride height loaded and unloaded — sagging beyond 1 inch below spec means springs are fatigued. 3) Check U-bolts for tightness — loose U-bolts allow axle wrap and spring damage. 4) Inspect the front suspension: Ball joints on IFS trucks, king pins on older solid-axle trucks. 5) On trucks with leveling kits: Inspect for accelerated ball joint and CV boot wear caused by altered suspension geometry. Repair Procedure: Leaf springs: Replace in pairs — add-a-leaf kits ($100-$200) for light increase, full replacement packs ($200-$500 per side) for worn springs. Torque U-bolts to spec (typically 60 ft-lbs for 9/16" bolts). Ball joints: Use a quality service kit with grease fittings. Press-in designs require a ball joint press ($50 rental). Level/lift kits: If installed incorrectly, re-index the pinion angle to prevent driveline vibration. Parts Needed: Leaf springs ($200-$500 per side), U-bolts ($30-$60), ball joints ($60-$120 each), helper springs or airbags for occasional heavy loads ($200-$400). Estimated Cost: $300-$1,500. Prevention: Don't exceed payload rating. Grease all fittings every 5,000 miles. Consider helper springs or airbag assist for frequent heavy loads.

title: Truck Battery — Dual-Battery & Heavy-Cranking System Service
content: Symptoms: Slow cranking (especially diesel trucks requiring high CCA), glow plug indicator staying on longer (diesel), auxiliary equipment losing power, batteries not holding charge overnight. Diagnostic Steps: 1) Diesel trucks often use dual batteries wired in parallel for high cranking amperage (1,000+ CCA total) — test BOTH batteries individually. A weak battery will drag down the good one. 2) Test the battery isolator/separator — it should connect batteries during charging and isolate them at rest. A failed isolator allows the weaker battery to drain the stronger one. 3) Check glow plug circuit (diesel) — faulty glow plugs increase cranking demand. 4) Measure parasitic draw — trucks with aftermarket work lights, inverters, and winches often have draws over 100mA. 5) Check for voltage drop across battery cables — corroded cables reduce cranking performance more than a weak battery. Repair Procedure: Replace batteries in pairs on dual-battery systems — mismatched batteries cause cross-charging and premature failure. Use batteries with the correct group size and CCA for the truck (typically Group 65 or 78, 750-850 CCA each). Clean and inspect all cable connections — replace cables showing green corrosion under the insulation. Test the isolator after installation. Parts Needed: Batteries ($150-$300 each), battery cables, terminal ends, battery isolator ($50-$150). Estimated Cost: $300-$800. Prevention: Replace both batteries at the same time. Keep terminals clean. Use a battery maintainer for trucks that sit idle. Test before winter.

title: Van Engine Overheating — Cargo & Passenger Van Cooling Service
content: Symptoms: Overheating in stop-and-go traffic (poor airflow at low speeds), rear heater losing output (passenger vans), coolant smell inside cabin (heater core leak), temperature rising when AC is on full. Diagnostic Steps: 1) Vans are prone to overheating due to restricted underhood airflow — check for debris blocking the radiator and AC condenser. 2) Passenger vans with rear heaters have additional coolant lines and a rear heater core — check for leaks at the rear connections under the van. 3) Inspect the serpentine belt and tensioner — a slipping belt reduces water pump and fan clutch performance. 4) Check the cooling fan(s) — vans typically rely heavily on electric fans due to transverse engine layouts. 5) Test the coolant concentration — vans in hot climates should run 50/50 coolant, but never exceed 70% coolant (reduces heat transfer). Repair Procedure: Replace cooling fan assembly (transverse engine vans typically have dual fans: one for radiator, one for AC condenser) — $150-$400. Flush cooling system including rear heater core lines — use a garden hose to backflush the rear heater core. Replace thermostat ($15-$30) and serpentine belt ($25-$50) as preventive measures during the repair. Parts Needed: Cooling fan assembly ($150-$400), thermostat, serpentine belt, coolant (2-3 gallons), radiator hoses. Estimated Cost: $200-$800. Prevention: Clean radiator fins annually with low-pressure water. Replace serpentine belt and tensioner every 60,000 miles. Idle with AC on when stuck in traffic to keep fans running.

title: Van Brake Service — Cargo Load & Passenger Safety
content: Symptoms: Longer stopping distances when loaded, brake pedal going lower than usual, rear brakes locking up when empty, warning lights, grinding noise. Diagnostic Steps: 1) Vans have variable loading — brakes must be inspected more frequently (every 15,000 miles) because weight varies drastically between empty and fully loaded. 2) Check the proportioning valve — cargo vans especially need proper front/rear bias adjustment. 3) Inspect rear drum brakes — many vans retain rear drums for cost and maintenance simplicity. Check shoe thickness (minimum 2mm), drum roundness, and wheel cylinder leaks. 4) Check brake hoses for swelling — flexible hoses on vans with sliding doors route through high-flex areas. 5) Verify the parking brake cable adjustment — cargo vans depend on the parking brake when loading/unloading on inclines. Repair Procedure: Replace pads/shoes with heavy-duty rated components. Vans hauling heavy cargo should use ceramic pads for consistent performance across temperature ranges. Service rear drums: Clean with brake cleaner, replace shoes, hardware springs, and wheel cylinders if leaking. Adjust star wheels until shoes lightly drag, then back off 10 clicks. Adjust parking brake cable for 4-6 clicks of travel. Parts Needed: Front brake pads ($40-$70), rear drum shoes ($30-$50), wheel cylinders ($20-$35 each), brake hardware kit, drums if out of round ($40-$70 each). Estimated Cost: $250-$600 per axle. Prevention: Inspect brakes every 15,000 miles regardless of feel. Adjust parking brake quarterly. Wash undercarriage to prevent corrosion on brake hardware.

title: Van Oil Leak — Accessibility Challenges & Common Sources
content: Symptoms: Oil on the garage floor (often hard to trace on vans due to engine covers and limited access), oil level dropping between changes, blue exhaust smoke on startup, oil smell through the cabin vents. Diagnostic Steps: 1) Van engine bays are notoriously cramped — use a borescope camera ($30-$50) to inspect the rear of the engine. 2) Common leak sources on transverse-mounted van engines: front crank seal (behind the accessory drive), valve cover gaskets (rear corner is impossible to see), and oil pressure sending unit. 3) On rear-engine vans (e.g., Sprinter types): Inspect the turbo oil lines and the oil filter housing gasket — these are the most common sources. 4) Check the intake manifold gasket — some van engines seep oil from the valley gasket. Repair Procedure: Due to limited access, many van oil leak repairs take significant labor. Valve cover gaskets: Remove intake components for access, clean sealing surfaces, apply RTV at critical points, torque in sequence. Oil pressure sending unit: Usually accessible from below — unscrew and replace with new O-ring ($10-$20 part). On rear-engine vans, turbo oil line replacement requires intake removal. Parts Needed: Valve cover gasket set ($20-$50), oil pressure sender ($15-$30), front crank seal ($10-$25), RTV sealant. Estimated Cost: $150-$800 (majority is labor due to access). Prevention: Use factory-spec oil viscosity. Don't skip oil changes. Inspect for leaks from below at every service since top-down inspection is difficult.

title: Van Transmission — High-Mileage Service & Rebuild Guide
content: Symptoms: Delayed shifts from stop (common on delivery vans), hunt-and-shift between gears on hills, shudder during torque converter lockup, overheating during heavy cargo hauling. Diagnostic Steps: 1) Check transmission fluid — vans used for delivery accumulate more heat cycles. Fluid should be replaced every 30,000 miles for commercial use. 2) Test the torque converter lockup — on highway, it should engage smoothly around 45 mph. A shudder indicates worn lockup clutch. 3) Scan for shift solenoid codes — high heat breaks down solenoid seals. 4) Check the transmission mount — van transmissions with worn mounts shift harshly due to excessive movement. 5) For vans with CVTs: Check for belt/chain whine and shudder during acceleration. Repair Procedure: Fluid and filter change with OEM-specified fluid. Install an external transmission cooler if not already equipped ($100-$200). Replace shift solenoids individually or as a pack ($200-$500). Torque converter replacement requires transmission removal ($600-$1,200). For a full rebuild on a high-mileage van, budget $2,000-$4,000. Parts Needed: ATF (10-14 quarts), filter, shift solenoid pack, external cooler, transmission mount ($40-$80). Estimated Cost: $200-$4,000. Prevention: Change fluid every 30,000 miles for commercial use. Install a transmission temperature gauge. Avoid exceeding payload ratings. Warm up before driving in cold weather.

title: Van Suspension — Commercial Load Support & Ride Quality
content: Symptoms: Rear sag when loaded (common on cargo vans), bottoming out over bumps, uneven ride height side to side, premature front tire wear, steering wheel off-center. Diagnostic Steps: 1) Measure ride height at all four corners loaded and unloaded — compare to manufacturer specs. 2) Inspect rear leaf springs/coil springs for sag and damage — cargo vans with constant heavy loads fatigue rear springs faster. 3) Check sway bar links and bushings — van bodies roll significantly due to high center of gravity, putting extra stress on stabilizer components. 4) Test shock absorbers: Look for oil leaks and perform bounce test. Cargo vans often wear out rear shocks in 30,000-40,000 miles. 5) Inspect steering components: Vans steer heavy loads and wear tie rod ends, idler arms, and pitman arms faster. Repair Procedure: Install heavy-duty rear springs rated for the cargo weight (upgrade from stock if consistently loading heavy). Add helper springs or air bag helpers for variable loads ($200-$400 kit). Replace worn shocks with commercial-grade units — gas-charged monotube shocks handle heat better. Replace sway bar links and bushings as a set. Parts Needed: Rear springs ($150-$400 per side), heavy-duty shocks ($50-$100 each), sway bar links ($25-$50 each), helper spring kit. Estimated Cost: $400-$1,200. Prevention: Don't exceed GVWR. Distribute weight evenly in the cargo area. Use load-rated tires. Inspect suspension at every oil change on commercial vans.

title: Van Battery — Auxiliary Systems & Dual-Battery Setup
content: Symptoms: Slow cranking especially with rear AC/heater running, auxiliary equipment (lifts, lighting) losing power, battery not charging to full, interior lights dimming at idle. Diagnostic Steps: 1) Passenger vans with rear climate control and cargo vans with power equipment draw heavily from the electrical system — test the stock alternator output vs. total accessory draw. 2) Check for a secondary/auxiliary battery (common on camper vans and work vans with lifts). Test both batteries independently. 3) Measure alternator output — should be 13.8-14.4V at idle with accessories on. Below this indicates a failing alternator or undersized unit. 4) Inspect the chassis-to-body ground straps — vans use long ground straps that corrode and create voltage drops. 5) Check the fuse box for signs of overheating — burned or melted fuse holders indicate circuits drawing excess current. Repair Procedure: Replace battery with correct group size — vans often use larger group sizes (Group 65 or 78) than sedans. If the vehicle has heavy accessory draw, upgrade to a higher-output alternator (180-250 amp) — $300-$600. Install a dual-battery system with an isolator for auxiliary equipment ($200-$400 install kit). Clean or replace chassis ground straps. Parts Needed: Battery ($120-$250), alternator ($200-$600), dual-battery isolator kit ($100-$200), ground straps ($20-$40). Estimated Cost: $150-$900. Prevention: Test the charging system quarterly on commercial vans. Keep auxiliary equipment off when the engine is not running. Install a battery disconnect switch for equipment that draws power when parked.

title: Van Tire Maintenance — Load Rating & Commercial Wear Patterns
content: Symptoms: Rapid inside-edge wear (overloaded), uneven wear front-to-rear (different loads per axle), sidewall cracking on tires that aren't driven much (age degradation), flat spots from sitting loaded. Diagnostic Steps: 1) Check the tire load rating against actual cargo weight — cargo vans must use Load Range D or E tires rated for the actual load. 2) Many van tires degrade from age before they wear out from use (UV exposure, sitting in sun). Check the DOT date code on the sidewall — tires over 6 years old should be replaced regardless of tread depth. 3) Inside-edge wear on front tires is common on vans due to the extra weight pushing alignment past toe specifications. 4) Check tire pressure loaded and unloaded — vans often need different pressures based on load. Repair Procedure: Replace tires with commercial-rated (C-rated) tires for cargo vans. Use LT (light truck) rated tires for passenger vans carrying full loads. After mounting, perform an alignment with the van at typical operating weight, not empty. Rotate tires every 5,000 miles — front tires on vans wear significantly faster due to steering loads and weight distribution. Parts Needed: Commercial van tires ($100-$250 each), valve stems, alignment. Estimated Cost: $500-$1,200 for a full set. Prevention: Rotate every 5,000 miles. Adjust pressure for load. Replace tires based on age (6-year max) even if tread remains. Park in shade to reduce UV degradation.

title: Bus Engine Overheating — Heavy-Duty Diesel Cooling System
content: Symptoms: Overheating under passenger load and AC demand, coolant loss through overflow, reduced power (derate mode), excessive coolant consumption without visible leak (head gasket). Diagnostic Steps: 1) Bus engines run under constant high load with AC — check the radiator core for blockage. Bus radiators are large and collect debris; clean with pressurized water from the engine side. 2) Test for combustion gas in the coolant using a block test kit (changes color from blue to yellow if head gasket is compromised). 3) Check the coolant additive level — heavy-duty diesel engines require SCAs (supplemental coolant additives) to prevent liner pitting. 4) Inspect the charge air cooler (intercooler) for leaks — pressurize to 30 PSI and listen/look for leaks. 5) Test the shutterstat or radiator shutter system — stuck-closed shutters restrict airflow. Repair Procedure: Flush and clean the cooling system with a radiator flush chemical. Replace coolant with correct heavy-duty formulation and SCA level. If the head gasket is blown, this is a major repair: remove the head, machine the surface, replace gaskets and head bolts ($2,000-$5,000). If the charge air cooler leaks, replace it ($500-$1,500). Parts Needed: Coolant (6-10 gallons), SCA additive, radiator cap, thermostat, charge air cooler, head gasket set if needed. Estimated Cost: $300-$6,000. Prevention: Test SCA levels at every service interval. Clean radiator quarterly. Monitor coolant level daily on commercial buses.

title: Bus Air Brake System — Inspection & Service Guide
content: Symptoms: Increased stopping distance, air pressure dropping below 90 PSI, automatic brake application warning, brake chambers leaking, air dryer not purging properly. Diagnostic Steps: 1) Buses use air brakes — check the air compressor cut-in (85 PSI) and cut-out (125 PSI) pressures. Adjust the governor if out of spec. 2) Perform a static leak test: With full air pressure and brakes released, pressure should not drop more than 2 PSI per minute. With brakes applied, no more than 3 PSI per minute. 3) Check slack adjuster free play — should be less than 1 inch of pushrod travel. 4) Inspect brake drums for cracks, heat checks, and out-of-round conditions. 5) Check the air dryer — a malfunctioning dryer allows moisture into the system, causing corrosion in valves and chambers. Drain the air tanks; if significant water exits, the dryer desiccant needs replacement. Repair Procedure: Adjust slack adjusters or replace automatic slack adjusters that are out of adjustment ($50-$100 each). Replace brake shoes with correct friction material for bus weight class. Re-line drums if within specifications or replace ($200-$400 each). Replace leaking brake chambers ($100-$200 each). Service the air dryer: Replace the desiccant cartridge ($80-$150). Parts Needed: Brake shoes ($60-$120 per axle), brake drums ($200-$400 each), slack adjusters ($50-$100 each), air dryer cartridge, brake chambers. Estimated Cost: $500-$3,000 for full service. Prevention: Drain air tanks daily. Service the air dryer annually. Inspect brake components at 10,000-mile intervals or more frequently for city buses.

title: Bus Engine Oil Leak — Heavy-Duty Diesel Diagnosis
content: Symptoms: Oil accumulation on the underside, oil level dropping between services (bus engines hold 15-30 quarts), oil dripping onto exhaust creating smoke and fire risk, oil on the DPF (diesel particulate filter). Diagnostic Steps: 1) Bus engines are large and have many potential leak points — use a systematic top-down approach. 2) Check the valve cover gaskets — vibration from large displacement engines loosens cover bolts over time. 3) Inspect the front and rear crankshaft seals — a front seal leak will throw oil onto the serpentine belt. 4) Check turbocharger oil drain tube — a kinked or blocked drain tube causes oil to push past the turbo seals into the intake or exhaust. 5) Inspect the oil pan — buses with low ground clearance in urban environments may have a damaged oil pan from speed bumps or debris. 6) Check the air compressor — the engine-driven air compressor has its own oil seals that can leak engine oil into the air system (causing oil contamination in the air system). Repair Procedure: Valve cover gaskets: Re-torque cover bolts to spec first. If still leaking, replace gaskets and ensure bolt threads are clean. Turbo drain tube: Replace if kinked. Clear any carbon blockage. Oil pan: Remove, clean, reseal with high-temp RTV, and re-torque. Parts Needed: Valve cover gasket set ($40-$100), oil pan gasket ($30-$75), turbo oil drain tube ($30-$60), front/rear crankshaft seals ($30-$80). Estimated Cost: $200-$2,000. Prevention: Re-torque valve covers every 50,000 miles. Check turbo drain tube during every oil change. Use manufacturer-specified heavy-duty diesel oil.

title: Bus Automatic Transmission — Allison & Heavy-Duty Diagnosis
content: Symptoms: Harsh shifts, delayed engagement from stop, transmission temperature alarm, slip in upper gears under load, limp mode (stuck in one gear). Diagnostic Steps: 1) Most buses use Allison automatic transmissions — connect an Allison DOC (Diagnostic Optimized Connection) tool to read codes and monitor live data. 2) Check the Allison TranSynd fluid level — these transmissions are sensitive to fluid level. Check when the transmission fluid temperature is between 160-200°F. 3) Test the transmission retarder — a non-functional retarder puts excessive load on service brakes. 4) Inspect the transmission cooler — check for restrictions by measuring inlet and outlet temperature differential (should be 10-30°F). 5) Check the output speed sensor — a failing sensor causes erratic shifting and can trigger limp mode. Repair Procedure: Allison transmissions require TranSynd or approved synthetic fluid — do NOT use conventional ATF. Fluid and filter change: Drain, replace both the main and lube filters, refill to correct level. For shift quality issues, replace the solenoid module ($400-$800). For major internal failure, Allison transmissions are typically rebuilt by specialist shops ($4,000-$8,000). Parts Needed: TranSynd fluid (28-30 quarts, $15-$20/quart), main filter, lube filter, solenoid module, gasket kit. Estimated Cost: $500-$8,000. Prevention: Use ONLY approved TranSynd or equivalent fluid. Change fluid and filters at 150,000-mile intervals (or 48 months). Monitor transmission temperature — should not exceed 250°F.

title: Bus Air Suspension — Ride Quality & Component Service
content: Symptoms: Vehicle sitting low on one side, air compressor running continuously, rough ride, bottoming out at stops (passenger loading), kneeling function not working. Diagnostic Steps: 1) Most modern buses use air suspension — check for audible air leaks at each air bag. Spray soapy water on air springs and lines to find leaks. 2) Test the ride height leveling valves — disconnect the linkage and manually actuate the valve. Air should flow to inflate/deflate the bag. 3) Check the ride height with a tape measure — compare left vs. right and front vs. rear. Maximum difference should be 0.5 inches. 4) Inspect air lines and fittings for cracks — UV exposure and vibration cause failures. 5) Test the kneeling system (passenger loading) — valve and solenoid operation. Repair Procedure: Replace air springs/bags ($100-$300 each) — jack up the axle, release air pressure, unbolt the old bag, clean the mounting surfaces, install the new bag, inflate and check for leaks. Replace ride height valves ($80-$200 each) if not controlling ride height properly. Repair air line leaks with DOT-approved push-to-connect fittings. Replace the air compressor rebuild kit ($100-$200) if the compressor can't maintain system pressure. Parts Needed: Air springs ($100-$300 each), ride height valves ($80-$200), air lines, push-to-connect fittings, compressor rebuild kit. Estimated Cost: $300-$2,000. Prevention: Inspect air bags and lines monthly. Keep the air system dry (maintain air dryer). Lubricate ride height valve linkages quarterly.

title: Bus Battery Bank — Multi-Battery System Diagnosis
content: Symptoms: Slow cranking of large diesel engine, inability to start after sitting overnight, auxiliary systems (lights, door, kneeling) not operating, battery disconnect switch malfunction. Diagnostic Steps: 1) Buses typically use 3-4 batteries wired in series/parallel (12V system) or 2 batteries in series (24V system) — test EACH battery individually with a load tester. 2) A single weak battery lowers the performance of the entire bank — all batteries should have CCA readings within 10% of each other. 3) Check the battery disconnect switch — corrosion in the switch adds resistance and reduces cranking performance. 4) Test the alternator output at idle and at 1,500 RPM with all accessories on — bus alternators should be 200+ amps. 5) Inspect the battery box for corrosion — battery acid fumes damage the tray and cables. Repair Procedure: Replace all batteries in the bank simultaneously — mixing old and new batteries causes the new ones to degrade quickly. Use heavy-duty commercial batteries rated for the application (typically Group 31, 950-1,100 CCA each). Clean and inspect the battery box — treat corrosion with baking soda solution, prime and paint with acid-resistant paint. Replace all corroded cables and lug terminals. Test the disconnect switch and replace if resistance exceeds 0.5 ohms. Parts Needed: Group 31 batteries ($200-$350 each, 3-4 needed), battery cables, lug terminals, disconnect switch, anti-corrosion spray. Estimated Cost: $800-$2,000. Prevention: Equalize charge the battery bank monthly. Keep battery tops clean and dry. Test all batteries every 3 months. Replace the entire bank every 3-4 years on commercial buses.

title: Bus Tire — Commercial Inspection & Replacement Protocol
content: Symptoms: Irregular wear patterns (common on steer axle), sidewall cracking and age degradation, casing damage from curb strikes, flat spots from heavy braking, low tire pressure warnings. Diagnostic Steps: 1) Bus tires must meet DOT commercial vehicle standards — minimum 4/32" tread depth on steer axle, 2/32" on drive axle. 2) Check tire pressure with a calibrated gauge — bus tires run 100-120 PSI and even a 10 PSI deviation significantly affects wear and safety. 3) Inspect for curb damage on the inside sidewall of steer tires — city buses frequently contact curbs. 4) Check the DOT date code — commercial bus tires should be removed from service at 7 years regardless of tread. 5) Inspect for retreads on the drive axle — retreads are acceptable on drive axles but NEVER on the steer axle. 6) Measure all tire diameters on dual-wheel setups — mismatched diameters cause one tire to scrub and overheat. Repair Procedure: Remove and replace tires meeting rejection criteria. Use premium steer-axle rated tires on the front. Balance wheels with wheel-mounted balancers (not stick-on weights which may fly off at high RPM). Torque lug nuts in a star pattern to 450-500 ft-lbs (typical for 10-hole hub-pilot wheels). Check torque after first 100 miles. Parts Needed: Commercial bus tires ($300-$600 each steer, $200-$400 drive), valve stems (metal cores required), hub seals. Estimated Cost: $1,500-$4,000 for a full set. Prevention: Check pressure daily (pre-trip). Rotate based on manufacturer schedule. Remove tires at 7 years max age. Train drivers to minimize curb contact.

title: Sedan AC System — Diagnosis & Recharge Guide
content: Symptoms: AC blowing warm air, intermittent cooling, unusual noise from compressor, visible refrigerant oil at fittings, AC clutch not engaging. Diagnostic Steps: 1) Connect manifold gauges to low and high side service ports — normal operating pressure: low side 25-45 PSI, high side 200-250 PSI. 2) Low on both sides: System has a leak. Use UV dye and blacklight to locate. Common leak points: condenser (road debris damage), evaporator (inside dash), schrader valves, hose crimps. 3) Low side too high, high side too low: Compressor not pumping — internal valve failure. 4) Check the compressor clutch: With AC on, the outer clutch plate should spin with the pulley. If not, check the clutch relay, pressure switch, and measure clutch air gap (0.015-0.025"). 5) Check the cabin air filter — a clogged filter restricts airflow and reduces cooling. Repair Procedure: If leak is found: Repair the leak, evacuate the system with a vacuum pump for 30 minutes minimum (to remove moisture), recharge with manufacturer-specified refrigerant amount (check the underhood sticker). Add correct amount of PAG oil based on the component replaced. If the compressor has failed, flush the condenser and hoses to remove metal debris before installing the new compressor. Parts Needed: Refrigerant R-134a ($10-$30 per can), compressor ($200-$500), condenser ($150-$300), receiver-drier ($30-$60), PAG oil. Estimated Cost: $100-$1,200. Prevention: Run the AC for 10 minutes at least once a month even in winter to keep seals lubricated. Replace the cabin air filter every 15,000 miles.

title: SUV AC System — Dual-Zone & Rear AC Diagnosis
content: Symptoms: Front AC works but rear doesn't (dual-zone systems), reduced cooling when all zones are running, compressor cycling rapidly, frost on the evaporator (expansion valve stuck open). Diagnostic Steps: 1) SUVs with dual or tri-zone AC have rear evaporators with separate expansion valves and blend doors — diagnose each zone independently. 2) Check the rear AC expansion valve — a stuck valve causes the rear evaporator to freeze and block airflow. 3) Measure refrigerant charge carefully — SUV dual-zone systems hold 50-100% more refrigerant than single-zone. Total system charge is critical (check underhood sticker). 4) Inspect the rear AC lines running under the vehicle — they are exposed to road debris and corrosion. 5) Test rear blend door actuators — a failed actuator defaults to heat, causing warm air from rear vents while the front works fine. Repair Procedure: For rear expansion valve: Access varies by vehicle — often requires removing rear trim panels. Replace the valve and add correct oil amount. For rear blend door actuator: Usually accessible by removing the rear trim panel. Replace the actuator motor ($50-$100) — test operation through the climate control before reassembly. For refrigerant leaks: Evacuate, repair, vacuum test, and recharge to exact specification. Parts Needed: Rear expansion valve ($40-$80), rear blend door actuator ($50-$100), refrigerant, rear AC lines if corroded ($80-$200). Estimated Cost: $200-$1,000. Prevention: Run rear AC monthly. Listen for blend door click at startup (normal operation). Inspect rear AC lines during oil changes for corrosion.

title: Truck Fuel System — Diesel Fuel Injection & Filter Service
content: Symptoms: Hard starting (especially in cold weather), rough idle, white smoke at startup (unburned fuel), black smoke under load (over-fueling), loss of power, fuel in the oil. Diagnostic Steps: 1) Change the fuel filter FIRST — diesel trucks are highly sensitive to fuel contamination. Water-in-fuel sensors should be monitored. 2) Check fuel pressure at the common rail — compare to manufacturer spec (typically 5,000-25,000 PSI depending on load). Low pressure indicates a failing high-pressure fuel pump or leaking injector. 3) Perform an injector balance test — this measures the contribution of each injector. A significantly different cylinder indicates a bad injector. 4) Inspect the fuel lines for air leaks — even a tiny air leak in the fuel supply line causes hard starting and rough idle. 5) On common rail diesel trucks: Check for injector return flow — excessive return flow indicates worn injector seals. Repair Procedure: Fuel filter replacement: Drain the water separator, replace both the primary and secondary filters, prime the system (use the hand primer pump or key-on cycle per manufacturer procedure). For injector replacement: Relieve fuel rail pressure, disconnect electrical and return lines, use the correct injector removal tool (they can be seized in the head). Install new injector copper sealing washers. For high-pressure pump: This is a precision component — replace with new or factory-rebuilt unit ($600-$1,500). Parts Needed: Fuel filters ($30-$80 for set), injectors ($200-$500 each), injector sealing washers, high-pressure pump ($600-$1,500). Estimated Cost: $100-$4,000. Prevention: Change fuel filters every 15,000 miles. Use premium diesel fuel. Add anti-gel treatment in cold weather. Drain water separator weekly.

title: Bus Electrical System — Multiplex Wiring & Body Control Diagnosis
content: Symptoms: Intermittent lighting failures, door system malfunctions, destination sign errors, wheelchair lift/ramp failures, HVAC control issues, CAN bus communication errors. Diagnostic Steps: 1) Modern buses use multiplexed wiring systems — diagnose using the vehicle's diagnostic interface (Cummins INSITE for engine, Allison DOC for transmission, body controller software for accessories). 2) Check for CAN bus communication faults — a single shorted node can take down the entire communication network. 3) Inspect the main power distribution panel — buses have high-amperage fuse blocks that corrode in the harsh under-bus environment. 4) Test ground circuits — buses are long vehicles and ground paths can develop high resistance, causing intermittent issues. 5) For door system issues: Check the air solenoid valve, actuator cylinder, and door interlock switch. For wheelchair lift issues: Check the hydraulic fluid level, pump motor, and safety interlocks. Repair Procedure: CAN bus issues: Use a multimeter to check CAN-Hi and CAN-Lo line resistance (should be approximately 60 ohms between them with all modules connected). Disconnect modules one at a time to isolate a shorted node. Lighting: Replace LED driver modules rather than individual LEDs. Clean and re-seal all exterior light connections with dielectric grease. Ground issues: Run a supplemental ground strap from the body to the chassis frame ($20-$50 fix for complex symptoms). Parts Needed: CAN bus node modules ($100-$500 each), LED driver modules ($50-$200), fuses and relays, ground straps, dielectric grease. Estimated Cost: $100-$2,000. Prevention: Inspect all electrical connections quarterly. Apply dielectric grease to all exterior connectors. Test all safety interlocks monthly.
